jokes.text: 'I love a hot BTS' / 'BTS to the BTS, baby!'
jokes.setup: The hosts are sincerely promoting a behind-the-scenes segment, using the abbreviation BTS
jokes.punchline: Ilana mishears or reinterprets 'BTS' as something sexually suggestive ('hot BTS'), then escalates it into nonsense ('BTS to the BTS, baby'), breaking the sincere promo tone
jokes.explanation: Classic Ilana move: take a neutral abbreviation and inject chaos/sexuality into it. Works as character comedy and subverts the promotional frame. Not especially original but efficiently deployed.

jokes.text: 'Dude, we are like Jerry Seinfeld right now. Yeah, we run this city.'
jokes.setup: They're in a car, feeling powerful, comparing themselves to a legendary New York comedian
jokes.punchline: The comparison to Seinfeld is immediately self-aggrandizing and absurd for two broke women who immediately proceed to fail at basic parking
jokes.explanation: Functions as meta-commentary on the show's own New York identity while also setting up the ironic deflation that follows. The self-comparison to Seinfeld is funny because of how quickly the episode proves it wrong.

jokes.text: 'We own you bitches! / The last guy was black so I didn't wanna, like, own him. Good call.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is on a power trip in the car, shouting 'We own you bitches!' at other drivers
jokes.punchline: She suddenly self-edits because the last driver was Black, catching herself on the word 'own' due to its slavery connotations — and Abbi simply says 'Good call'
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. Only Ilana — a character defined by her performative but earnest social justice awareness — would catch herself mid-bluster and make this specific edit, and only Abbi would deadpan 'Good call' as if it's a perfectly reasonable mid-sentence correction. The joke does what great Broad City comedy does: it's raunchy and subversive while simultaneously satirizing white liberal self-congratulation. The economy is perfect — the whole thing lands in two sentences.

jokes.text: The parking spot turns out to be too small; Abbi and Ilana curse each other out then concede 'Okay, it's too small, anyway'
jokes.setup: They're thrilled to find a parking spot and celebrate
jokes.punchline: The spot is too small; after bleeping each other out they immediately rationalize it as 'too small, anyway' — classic sour-grapes denial
jokes.explanation: Functional sitcom beat. The bleep-out fight is energetic but the sour-grapes rationalization is a familiar comedic move. Carries momentum but doesn't distinguish itself.

jokes.text: 'There are no spots. Does everyone have a car now?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is frustrated after losing multiple parking spots
jokes.punchline: Her complaint captures authentic NYC exasperation — a city where car ownership is genuinely baffling — delivered with perfect defeated energy
jokes.explanation: A nicely observed NYC-specific line. Short, real, and lands on the feeling of being the only responsible person in a city full of newly car-owning idiots.

jokes.text: 'Owning this car's been the worst two weeks of my life.' / 'Wait, someone's gonna steal it.' / 'Exactly.'
jokes.setup: Abbi laments owning the car; Ilana points out it might be stolen
jokes.punchline: Abbi says 'Exactly' — theft is the best possible outcome at this point
jokes.explanation: Tight, well-structured joke. The single word 'Exactly' does all the work. It reframes insurance fraud/theft as a solution rather than a threat, which is a clean misdirection payoff. Good economy.

jokes.text: 'All Dunkaroos are expired. Any Dunkaroos you see are literally from the '90s. They're from before I was born.'
jokes.setup: Abbi tries to return expired Dunkaroos to a bodega
jokes.punchline: Ilana's aside that ALL Dunkaroos are ancient artifacts — not a product anyone manufactures currently — and her addition that they predate her own birth
jokes.explanation: Surprisingly sharp. The joke works on multiple levels: it's true (Dunkaroos basically vanished after the 90s), it's delivered as authoritative trivia by Ilana, and 'from before I was born' escalates to just the right degree of absurdist self-insert. Culturally specific in a way that rewards the audience.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, you can't return stuff at a bodega.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has launched into a full return request at a bodega as if it's a department store
jokes.punchline: The owner responds with total flat calm, stating the obvious that should have prevented this entire conversation
jokes.explanation: The straight-man response lands well after Abbi's escalating absurdity. One sentence, maximum deflation. The real joke is how long it took to arrive.

jokes.text: 'Okay, well then I will please take store credit.'
jokes.setup: She's just been told you can't return things at a bodega
jokes.punchline: Rather than accepting this and leaving, Abbi escalates to requesting store credit — at a bodega — as if she's at Nordstrom
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke. Abbi's persistence is character-specific — she's the slightly more rule-following one trying to work within systems even when those systems don't apply. The 'store credit at a bodega' concept is genuinely funny.

jokes.text: 'You can return them if you can tell me my name.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been pestering the bodega owner for a return/store credit
jokes.punchline: The owner invents an absurd condition — tell me my name — knowing Abbi doesn't know it, as a polite way to end the negotiation
jokes.explanation: Clever and character-rich. The bodega owner has figured out she can end this ridiculous negotiation by exposing Abbi's failure to know her name — something Abbi clearly doesn't know. It plants the seed for the episode's recurring 'name' thread. Smart, economical, and sets up later payoffs.

jokes.text: Abbi confidently starts 'Your name is Bo...' and trails off, then tries 'Bodega' as a name
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to recall the owner's name; she starts with 'Bo...' implying she knows it
jokes.punchline: The best she can produce is 'Bodega' — she has literally named the woman after the store
jokes.explanation: Genuinely cringe-funny. Abbi's social shame of not knowing the name of someone she clearly knows combines with the accidental insult of 'Bodega.' The owner's reaction ('You think my name is Bodega?') makes it land hard. Good cringe escalation.

jokes.text: 'Bo... dine. That's not a name. It is so. My favorite aunt's name is Bodine.'
jokes.setup: Abbi tries to recover from calling the owner 'Bodega' by claiming she was going to say 'Bodine'
jokes.punchline: When challenged that 'Bodine' isn't a name, Ilana immediately produces a fake aunt named Bodine to validate Abbi's cover story
jokes.explanation: Ilana's reflexive loyalty in immediately fabricating 'Aunt Bodine' to back up Abbi's obvious lie is perfect best-friend comedy. It escalates the absurdity while being completely in character.

jokes.text: 'Bye, Abbi.' — the bodega owner knows Abbi's name but Abbi never knew hers
jokes.setup: The bodega owner has watched Abbi fumble her name completely
jokes.punchline: She casually says 'Bye, Abbi' — demonstrating she knows Abbi's name perfectly, highlighting the one-sided nature of their relationship
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the bodega sequence and one of the best in the episode. The economy is exceptional — two words do everything. It's a perfect structural callback that also functions as a pointed social commentary: the small business owner who serves Abbi regularly knows her name, while Abbi has never learned hers. It's funny AND slightly stinging in its accuracy about how people interact with service workers. Could only land because of the setup it was given.

jokes.text: Bye, Abbi.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just lectured the bodega owner about being 'very irresponsible' for selling expired products
jokes.punchline: The owner knows Abbi's name — has always known it — and drops it with flat finality, completely reversing the power dynamic and exposing Abbi's obliviousness in two words
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. Two words do everything: they retroactively reframe the entire scene, expose Abbi's casual dehumanization of service workers, puncture her self-righteous lecture, and make the bodega owner the most dignified person in the scene. The structure is near-perfect — the premise was set up two full beats ago, the audience may have half-forgotten it, and then it lands with total economy. It's a callback, a reversal, a character indictment, and a mic-drop, all in two words. Justifies the 8.7 craft score.

jokes.text: Ilana measuring the armoire dimensions while Abbi is trying to get ready for work — 'Can you show me the dimensions of your armoire?' 'What? Just do it!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is rushing to get ready; Ilana is apparently conducting furniture measurements with no explanation
jokes.punchline: The complete mismatch of priorities — Ilana treating armoire dimensions as urgent while Abbi's trying to get to work — is funny through sheer non-sequitur energy
jokes.explanation: Funny in the moment as a character-dynamics beat — Ilana's chaotic priority list invading Abbi's morning — but underdeveloped as a standalone joke. Works as setup for the dog reveal.

jokes.text: Visual gag: Ilana reveals a dog she has apparently smuggled or obtained secretly — Abbi reacts with shock ('What?')
jokes.setup: Ilana is asking Abbi about armoire dimensions, which seems like a mundane decorating question
jokes.punchline: The reveal that the armoire measurement was for a dog — she's obtained a dog and hidden it
jokes.explanation: A classic misdirection visual reveal. 'What are the dimensions of your armoire' is a perfect decoy question. The dog payoff is very Ilana — impulsive, chaotic, tender. Works better on screen than on page.

jokes.text: Ilana talking-head: 'I always thought of being a dog uncle. Coming in and out of the dog's life, all about fun and games...' vs. 'Being a dog dad is different. It's a lot of responsibility.'
jokes.setup: Ilana describes her previous dog philosophy as an avuncular 'dog uncle'
jokes.punchline: The shift to 'dog dad is different' applies the language of serious parental reckoning to dog ownership — Ilana performing adult responsibility she clearly does not have
jokes.explanation: Solid talking-head joke. The contrast between Ilana's genuine earnestness about being a 'dog dad' and her chaotic personality is a good character-specific irony. Middling originality but well executed.

jokes.text: 'You know what's also a big choice? Killing a dog, and that's what was gonna happen to her today. But, you know, I get it. If you feel a six-figure salary isn't enough to support a tiny dog...'
jokes.setup: Someone (Lincoln, presumably) is questioning whether Ilana should have a dog
jokes.punchline: Ilana guilt-trips with 'you were basically going to kill this dog' then immediately undercuts her righteousness with a sarcastic dig about six-figure salaries
jokes.explanation: Good Ilana manipulation. She pivots from guilt-tripping to sarcastic class commentary seamlessly. The 'six-figure salary' dig at Lincoln (a dentist) is very on-brand.

jokes.text: The 'I'm actually an artist... showing in New York and London right now' exchange with the can-recycling worker
jokes.setup: Abbi is recycling bottles and cans for cash; she feels the need to assert her artistic identity to a fellow recycler
jokes.punchline: The recycler casually one-ups her: 'Me too. Showing in New York and London right now.' Abbi can only respond that she's showing 'in one of the most prestigious chiropractor's offices in the Village'
jokes.explanation: Sharp joke about NYC artistic hierarchies and Abbi's aspirational self-image. The recycler being a legitimate international artist is a perfect subversion. 'Prestigious chiropractor's office in the Village' as Abbi's best comeback is wonderfully self-deflating. Captures the NYC experience of being humbled by random strangers.

jokes.text: Ilana gives Jaime a refurbished iPad as a gift; he notes it's not in a box; she explains 'It's like brand new. Except not.'
jokes.setup: Ilana presents the iPad as a generous gift
jokes.punchline: 'It's like brand new. Except not.' — a perfect non-defense of a refurbished gift
jokes.explanation: A nice Ilana line. The blunt tautological logic ('brand new, except not') is characteristically Ilana — she's generous but also utterly unapologetic about the terms of that generosity.

jokes.text: Jaime lectures Ilana about gift-giving etiquette after she gave him a refurbished iPad: 'One could make the argument that if you give a gift, it should be brand new'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just given Jaime a $400 refurbished iPad
jokes.punchline: Jaime immediately launches into a lecture about gift-giving standards — a perfectly entitled response to a free iPad
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Jaime — his deadpan presumption is funny. Not a strong standalone joke but works in context.

jokes.text: 'Now, you gotta get out of here because Abbi's coming and I want her to think she's the only person getting a gift today.' / 'Is hers new?' / 'Get out of here. Go!'
jokes.setup: Ilana is rushing Jaime out before Abbi arrives — so Abbi will think she's the only gift recipient
jokes.punchline: Jaime immediately asks 'Is hers new?' — cutting to the real competitive question, which Ilana refuses to answer
jokes.explanation: Tight comedic exchange. 'Is hers new?' is the perfect Jaime question — petty, precise, and cutting. The non-answer from Ilana confirms everything.

jokes.text: 'My train karma is at an all-time low, as is my general karma.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is apologizing for being late
jokes.punchline: She expands 'train karma' into 'general karma' — a small escalation that suggests her problems go well beyond the MTA
jokes.explanation: Functional self-deprecating line that's relatable. The upgrade from transit-specific to cosmic-scale bad luck is a nice small escalation.

jokes.text: 'How many dumps have I taken at Barnes and Noble?' as a job qualification
jokes.setup: Abbi lists Barnes and Noble as a potential job to apply to
jokes.punchline: Ilana's non-sequitur about using Barnes and Noble bathrooms implies she's essentially a regular — treating the store as a public utility — and frames it as relevant to employment prospects
jokes.explanation: Gloriously Ilana. The non-sequitur is perfectly timed, the oversharing is on-brand, and there's an implicit absurdist logic that bathroom usage = familiarity = employability. Raunchy without being gratuitous.

jokes.text: The expensive bag reveal — Ilana gifts Abbi a $1,300 bag; Abbi spirals: 'Was it more than the blue dress? Was it more than an ounce? Was it more than my rent?'
jokes.setup: Ilana presents a fancy bag as a gift; Abbi is touched but nervous about the price
jokes.punchline: Abbi's price-guessing escalation includes 'an ounce' as a benchmark — using drug quantity as a financial reference point — and peaks at 'my rent... Times two'
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific escalation. 'Was it more than an ounce?' as a price reference is very Broad City — Abbi's financial frame of reference runs through rent, drugs, and clothing in that order. The 'Times two' gut-punch at the end is well-structured.

jokes.text: Ilana's speech about dressing for success: 'You need to look how you feel.' / 'I feel like I spent two hours this morning recycling bottles and cans for $4.15.'
jokes.setup: Ilana delivers an inspirational 'dress for success' speech
jokes.punchline: Abbi's dead-accurate self-assessment undercuts the whole thing — she does not feel like a $1,300 bag person, she feels like someone who made $4.15 recycling this morning
jokes.explanation: The callback to the recycling scene makes this land harder. Ilana's motivational framework vs. Abbi's brutal honesty about her actual morning is a perfect Broad City comedic tension. '$4.15' is a beautifully specific detail.

jokes.text: 'Well, now you can feel like you wear two months' rent around your body.'
jokes.setup: Abbi says she feels like someone who spent the morning recycling cans for $4.15
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response is to reframe the bag as a wearable financial instrument — 'two months' rent around your body' is both horrifying and aspirational
jokes.explanation: A good Ilana line. 'Wear two months' rent around your body' is a vivid, specific reframe that captures both the obscenity of luxury goods and Ilana's genuine love for Abbi.

jokes.text: Ilana calling Jaime 'my little Guatemalan coffee bean'
jokes.setup: Ilana approaches Jaime with a gift
jokes.punchline: The nickname 'my little Guatemalan coffee bean' is simultaneously affectionate and cringe-inducing — Ilana's progressive self-image in direct tension with her tone-deaf pet names
jokes.explanation: Works as character comedy precisely because it encapsulates Ilana's particular brand of well-meaning but ethnicity-fixated affection. Not a big laugh but adds texture.

jokes.text: Jaime's theatrical 'We have bed bugs!' announcement / Ilana's extended scream
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives home to find Jaime distressed
jokes.punchline: The revelation is delivered with maximum dramatic performance — both Jaime's announcement and Ilana's sustained scream are far beyond the emotional proportionality of the situation, making the absurdity of the reaction the joke
jokes.explanation: Works better as a visual/performance moment than as written comedy. The screaming is classic sitcom physical escalation. Functional and energetic.

jokes.text: Ilana and Jaime's landlord/exterminator arrival — the woman sees Ilana's designer bag and says 'Love that bag'
jokes.setup: Ilana is in crisis over bed bugs; a professional arrives presumably to help
jokes.punchline: The exterminator's first comment is about the bag — completely ignoring the emergency to compliment fashion — which also sets up the bag becoming part of the bed bug plot
jokes.explanation: Functional setup beat that plants the bag as a plot thread. The exterminator ignoring the bed bug crisis to comment on fashion is a small but effective tonal joke.

jokes.text: 'People like us don't get bed bugs!' / 'Yes, we can. Because we do.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is in denial about having bed bugs
jokes.punchline: Her class-inflected protest ('people like us') is immediately and logically demolished by Jaime
jokes.explanation: Good irony — Ilana who constantly performs class consciousness suddenly invokes class privilege when it's personally inconvenient. Jaime's flat correction is perfect.

jokes.text: 'I've been going through a dry spell ever since I cut off my peepee.' / 'We got this from jou, lady.' / 'Jew lady?' / 'You! You!'
jokes.setup: Ilana and Jaime are arguing about who gave whom bed bugs
jokes.punchline: 'Jou' (Jaime's accent for 'you') is misheard by Ilana as 'Jew' — a double-take on accent vs. anti-Semitism — resolved when Jaime clarifies 'You! You!'
jokes.explanation: Sharp wordplay that's also character-specific — only Ilana, a Jewish woman, would immediately clock 'jou' as a possible slur and need it corrected. It's quick, surprising, and earns a genuine laugh. The setup about 'cutting off my peepee' is also a funny throwaway.

jokes.text: 'Well, I am in a relationship so I'm safe.'
jokes.setup: They're discussing where the bed bugs came from — who brought them in
jokes.punchline: Ilana non-sequiturs into relationship-as-STI-protection logic — conflating bed bugs with sexual transmission and monogamy with safety
jokes.explanation: A fun Ilana conflation of sex and pest infestation. It's absurd logic delivered with total sincerity, which is her best mode.

jokes.text: 'Okay, then maybe we got them from Abbi.' / 'Don't you dare bring her into this!' / 'I went too far.'
jokes.setup: Ilana deflects blame from herself by suggesting it came from someone else
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately pivots to fierce loyalty when Abbi is mentioned — then Jaime's immediate self-aware confession 'I went too far' is a perfect capper
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy. Ilana's instantaneous switch from 'blame everyone' to 'not ABBI' is a sharp illustration of her priorities. Jaime's self-aware 'I went too far' — spoken immediately, without prompting — is a comedic beat that would work in a sketch, a film, or here.

jokes.text: 'Have you been using protection?' / 'What are we even talking about now?' / 'A mattress protector.'
jokes.setup: The exterminator asks about protection
jokes.punchline: The obvious sexual reading of 'protection' is set up by the conversation's tone, then deflated — she means mattress protectors, a bed bug prevention measure
jokes.explanation: Classic double-entendre misdirection, competently executed. Works but it's a familiar structure. The best part is Ilana's 'what are we even talking about now?' which shows she's been genuinely lost in the conversation.

jokes.text: 'Tell me you got a mattress protector.' / 'Yes.' / 'Y-yes.' / 'You lying. You are a liar! Mattress protector!'
jokes.setup: The exterminator asks if they used a mattress protector — an obvious and basic precaution
jokes.punchline: Both hesitate, then lie unconvincingly, and the exterminator's blunt accusation ('You are a liar!') explodes into absurd intensity over a mattress cover
jokes.explanation: The exterminator's disproportionate outrage at the lie is the best element here. Good escalation beat, nothing transcendent.

jokes.text: God, maybe I could've fucked less people before I got back together with Lincoln. Those people were filthy.
jokes.setup: The exterminator is giving the bed bug speech, saying it's not their fault and bugs don't discriminate
jokes.punchline: Ilana's private thought-cloud: she's doing the math on her sexual history as a possible vector — 'Those people were filthy' is a deeply unfair retrospective judgment delivered with total self-interest
jokes.explanation: 'Those people were filthy' after a vague reference to sleeping with many people is very Ilana — judgmental about her own former partners, self-exonerating, and delivered as a completely private observation rather than anything publicly appropriate.

jokes.text: Exterminator: 'I once saw these suckers take out the Metropolitan Opera House.'
jokes.setup: The exterminator is trying to make them feel better about bed bugs
jokes.punchline: Her reassurance is that bed bugs have defeated even the most prestigious institutions — the Metropolitan Opera — which is both darkly funny and completely unhelpful
jokes.explanation: A well-chosen specific NYC institution makes this land. 'The Metropolitan Opera House' is exactly the right prestige target — high culture leveled by a pest. Good deadpan from the exterminator.

jokes.text: I always sit on the wooden benches in the subway platform even though I know that they are carriers. I just love the way the wood feels.
jokes.setup: Both are confessing possible sources of bed bugs
jokes.punchline: Jaimé's confession reveals both his contribution to their problem AND a minor fetish for wooden bench textures — the 'I just love the way the wood feels' is a bizarrely sensual detail about subway infrastructure
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jaimé character joke. 'I just love the way the wood feels' is wonderfully specific and weird — it combines a confession, an irrational risk-taking behavior, and a sensory preference that makes the confession stranger. The specificity of 'wooden benches' is the right detail. High character integration.

jokes.text: 'What if we got rid of our clothes and our possessions? And what if we promised to learn how to pray?'
jokes.setup: They're trying to figure out how to deal with the bed bugs
jokes.punchline: Ilana's solution escalates from practical ('get rid of stuff') to existential ('learn how to pray') — suggesting bed bugs have triggered a spiritual crisis
jokes.explanation: The pivot to prayer as a bed bug response is a good escalation that characterizes Ilana's chaos-brain. Funny in its specific escalation pattern.

jokes.text: 'You don't treat the apartment and move and the city finds out, you're looking at ten years to life.'
jokes.setup: They consider moving instead of treating the bed bugs
jokes.punchline: The exterminator describes a bed bug cover-up as if it were a serious criminal offense — 'the city finds out, you're looking at ten years to life'
jokes.explanation: Very strong joke. The exterminator plays the NYC bed bug ecosystem as if it were a mob underworld — complete with criminal penalties for violating protocol. It's absurdist but grounded in a very real NYC paranoia about bed bug stigma. 'Ten years to life' for not treating an apartment is a perfect absurdist escalation delivered in perfect deadpan.

jokes.text: 'This moist, dark paper right here. That's where they first laid their eggs.' The paper is Ilana's cash.
jokes.setup: The exterminator has found the source of the infestation
jokes.punchline: The source is Ilana's money — described as 'moist, dark paper' — implying Ilana keeps her cash in conditions perfect for bug nesting
jokes.explanation: Good visual joke — the word 'moist' alone does work here. The implication that Ilana's cash is so damp and dark it attracted bed bugs is a nice character-specific gross-out detail. The visual of someone's money being called a pest habitat is memorable.

jokes.text: 'Isn't that illegal under federal law?' / 'When it comes to bed bugs, it's illegal not to burn infested cash.'
jokes.setup: Ilana questions whether burning money is illegal
jokes.punchline: The exterminator inverts the law: in BED BUG WORLD, it's illegal NOT to burn infested cash — an absurdist escalation of the earlier 'ten years to life' joke
jokes.explanation: Extends the bit about the exterminator's parallel legal universe. Consistent with the established absurdist rules of her character. Good but slightly less fresh than the original.

jokes.text: 'Why couldn't we have just gotten herpes? Then we could at least keep all our stuff and I could've just learned to use concealer.'
jokes.setup: They're being told they have to burn their possessions because of bed bugs
jokes.punchline: Ilana laments that herpes would have been preferable — because at least with herpes you keep your furniture
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and one of the better jokes Broad City ever wrote. It works on every level: it's wildly original (no one has ever made this comparison before), structurally perfect (the 'concealer' kicker is unexpected and extends the joke an extra beat beyond where you expect it to end), and completely character-specific — only Ilana would have the casual attitude toward STIs necessary to make this comparison, and only she would have the practical-beauty-tips-inflected afterthought about concealer. The logic is internally consistent: bed bugs = lose everything; herpes = keep everything + learn makeup. It's absurdist but grounded in actual consequences. Highly quotable, rewatchable, and the kind of line that ends up in clip compilations.

jokes.text: 'I can't guarantee that I can get 'em out. However, I can guarantee that I can't.'
jokes.setup: After everything, the exterminator delivers her professional assessment
jokes.punchline: Her guarantee is that she can guarantee failure — a perfectly useless and yet somehow reassuring statement that undermines the entire service she's providing
jokes.explanation: An exceptionally well-constructed line. The structure — 'I can't guarantee success, but I can guarantee failure' — is logically paradoxical in a satisfying way. It's also a perfect encapsulation of the NYC service industry experience. High economy: every word is doing work.

jokes.text: Abbi's Anthropologie interview tangent: 'The flagship Anthropologie was in my hometown' / 'I probably knew about Anthropologie before you did' / 'I turned a shitty job into a fitty job'
jokes.setup: Abbi is in a job interview at Anthropologie; she's trying to present herself as impressive and connected to the brand
jokes.punchline: She competitively asserts Anthropologie brand knowledge, then the interviewer drops 'I turned a shitty job into a fitty job' — a painfully forced rhyme attempting to make 'fitness trainer' sound like a corporate success story
jokes.explanation: 'Fitty job' is a great cringe wordplay moment. The whole interview sequence plays on the unbearable intimacy of interview small talk and Abbi's eagerness to connect over brand heritage. Moderate laughs throughout.

jokes.text: Marcel reveals Sushi Mambeaux is closed because 'Brenda was running a prostitution ring out of Sushi Mambeaux after hours'
jokes.setup: Ilana calls her restaurant job to figure out what's happening
jokes.punchline: The reason the restaurant is closed for fumigation is that a coworker was running a full prostitution ring from the premises after hours — a massive escalation from 'bed bugs' to 'sex work and who knows what else'
jokes.explanation: A good escalation reveal. The prostitution ring is an absurdist explanation for the bed bugs that also lands as satirical commentary on the restaurant industry's chaos. Marcel's delivery style makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Brenda was running a prostitution ring out of Sushi Mambeaux after hours. She brought in bed bugs and who in the heck knows what else.
jokes.setup: Ilana calls Sushi Mambeaux to find out about the source of the bed bugs
jokes.punchline: Marcel casually reveals the restaurant has been used as an after-hours prostitution ring — which explains the bed bugs and is treated as an administrative inconvenience rather than a crime
jokes.explanation: The prostitution ring as backstory is a great escalation — it explains the bed bugs and adds to the episode's running STI/pest conflation gag. 'Who in the heck knows what else' delivered in Marcel's signature register is funny.

jokes.text: 'Child, I hope you put your cash in the bank and made yourself a little breast egg 'cause who knows if we'll bounce back.'
jokes.setup: Marcel is telling Ilana the restaurant might not reopen
jokes.punchline: 'Breast egg' instead of 'nest egg' — a malapropism delivered with total confidence by Marcel, who also uses 'bounce back' where he means 'come back'
jokes.explanation: 'Breast egg' is a great malapropism — plausibly wrong in the way a second-language or idiosyncratic speaker might get it wrong. Marcel's language is consistently a source of humor and this is one of his better lines.

jokes.text: Child, I hope you put your cash in the bank and made yourself a little breast egg.
jokes.setup: Marcel tells Ilana the restaurant is closing indefinitely
jokes.punchline: 'Breast egg' instead of 'nest egg' — a malapropism that is either Marcel's error or deliberate, and either way is funnier than 'nest egg'
jokes.explanation: 'Breast egg' is a perfectly deployed malapropism — it's one letter different, sounds plausible in Marcel's affected register, and is funnier than the real phrase. Marcel as a character thrives on this kind of linguistic exuberance.

jokes.text: 'I'm gonna go wherever I can be the best father to my only son. Well, my son.'
jokes.setup: Marcel is explaining where he'll go now that the restaurant is closed
jokes.punchline: 'My only son. Well, my son.' — the self-correction implies there may be more than one son, and he doesn't want to imply exclusivity
jokes.explanation: A tiny, beautifully constructed joke. The self-correction does enormous character work in three words — suddenly Marcel has a complicated family situation implied entirely by the edit. Good economy.

jokes.text: 'It's 'cause I'm sweeter.' (re: why Ilana has it worse with bed bugs)
jokes.setup: Jaime notes Ilana has it worse than him with bed bugs
jokes.punchline: Ilana explains this is because she's literally sweeter — as in, the bugs prefer her blood because she is a more delectable person
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ilana — she reframes a negative (bugs prefer her) as a positive (because she's the sweetest). Very economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: We are fully fucked by bed bugs.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Jaimé despair over their situation
jokes.punchline: 'Fully fucked by bed bugs' — the sexual connotation applied to insect infestation continues the episode's running STI metaphor, but also just lands as a great turn of phrase
jokes.explanation: A good phrase that serves as an episode thesis statement while continuing the sex/bugs conflation. Ilana's phrasing is always slightly dirtier than necessary.

jokes.text: Bodega owner says 'Nice purse' to Abbi — as the mugger's stolen bag is now somehow associated with Abbi at the bodega
jokes.setup: Abbi has just been mugged and possibly lost the bag; she arrives at the bodega
jokes.punchline: The bodega owner commenting on the purse — right after the mugging sequence — adds ironic twist to the bag's journey
jokes.explanation: Functional callback that keeps the bag thread alive. Simple but effective placement.

jokes.text: Nice purse. (Abbi looks down at the expensive bag, then back up, horrified at what's about to happen)
jokes.setup: Abbi has been mugged at gunpoint; has just retrieved her cash
jokes.punchline: The mugger's compliment on the bag signals he's going to take it — the audience and Abbi both understand this simultaneously, and her reaction beat is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Nice purse' is perfectly economical — two words that communicate inevitability. The callback to the bag's cost makes this hit harder. Abbi's unspoken reaction is the full joke.

jokes.text: The mugging sequence: The mugger gives Abbi financial advice while robbing her at gunpoint
jokes.setup: Abbi is being mugged; the mugger demands her ATM balance
jokes.punchline: Upon seeing her balance of $374, the mugger abandons the mugging to lecture Abbi on emergency savings, the economy tanking, and keeping more money in her account — becoming her unsolicited financial advisor
jokes.explanation: This is an extended comedic premise with strong sustained escalation. The concept — mugger appalled by victim's finances and pivoting to financial advice — is original and well-executed. Key beats: the mugger noting her 1988 birthday PIN, the shock at $374, the advice about emergencies and the economy tanking, and Abbi's perfect comeback 'You're giving me advice? You're mugging me right now.' Each beat within this sequence escalates naturally. The sequence doesn't quite reach the 'herpes' joke level of brilliance but it's the strongest sustained comedic sequence in the episode.

jokes.text: Let me guess. Were you born in 1-9-88? Jesus. Good PIN.
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to enter her PIN with a gun pointed at her
jokes.punchline: The mugger immediately guesses her birth year PIN — 'Jesus. Good PIN.' — which is simultaneously a critique of her security and an in-the-moment compliment that is deeply absurd
jokes.explanation: The 'Good PIN' sarcasm mid-mugging is very funny — the mugger taking a moment to appreciate Abbi's terrible security choice. 'Jesus' before 'Good PIN' is the right rhythm.

jokes.text: 'Oh my God. Is this a joke?' / 'No, I'm having a rough time right now.' (re: $374 balance)
jokes.setup: The mugger sees Abbi's bank balance
jokes.punchline: Abbi's 'I'm having a rough time right now' as a response to being mugged — she's apologizing to her mugger for her financial situation
jokes.explanation: Abbi apologizing to her mugger for her low balance is a perfectly calibrated cringe joke. Her instinct toward social grace even in a robbery is completely on-character — she's the type who apologizes to everyone including people committing crimes against her. Short and very funny.

jokes.text: 'What if the economy all of a sudden tanks? What're you gonna do?' — mugger giving financial advice at gunpoint
jokes.setup: The mugger is appalled by Abbi's savings
jokes.punchline: He delivers a legitimate concern about economic downturns while still pointing a gun at her — the juxtaposition of macroeconomic anxiety and immediate criminal threat
jokes.explanation: The economic anxiety advice from an active mugger is the absurdist core of the sequence. Works well as a standalone beat within the larger mugging scene.

jokes.text: 'You're giving me advice? You're mugging me right now so...'
jokes.setup: The mugger is lecturing Abbi about financial responsibility
jokes.punchline: Abbi points out the obvious — this man is robbing her while advising her — with perfect flat exasperation
jokes.explanation: The best line of the mugging sequence and a contender for episode top-five. The flatness of 'so...' trailing off is perfect deadpan. She's not even angry — just stating the obvious contradiction with mild exasperation. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'At least I'm proactive about my finances.' — mugger, mid-robbery
jokes.setup: Abbi has called out the mugger's hypocrisy
jokes.punchline: He defends himself by claiming financial proactivity — mugging as a proactive financial strategy
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'At least I'm proactive about my finances' reframes mugging as a hustle — a perverse but internally logical financial philosophy. It's original, character-specific to this one-off character, and highly quotable. The mugger has become a coherent comedic character in about 90 seconds.

jokes.text: Mugger then questions the expensive bag: 'You clearly are living beyond your means. Can you afford that bag? That's too expensive for you. Give me that.'
jokes.setup: The mugger has pivoted to being Abbi's financial counselor
jokes.punchline: His financial advice concludes with him taking the bag — which is both absurdist logic (he assessed it as unaffordable) and also still a mugging
jokes.explanation: The bag theft lands as both a callback payoff and an extension of the mugger's internal logic — he's not stealing it, he's performing a service by removing it from someone who can't afford it. The irony is thick: Abbi was right to be worried about the bag's cost; even her mugger agrees. Good structural payoff.

jokes.text: The bodega owner shoots the mugger's gun away / 'Get out of my bodega, motherfucker'
jokes.setup: The mugger has stolen Abbi's bag; Abbi is helpless
jokes.punchline: The bodega owner, who has been present the whole time, casually shoots the gun out of the mugger's hand and ejects him with one authoritative line
jokes.explanation: The bodega owner's transformation into action hero is a great payoff for her earlier Second Amendment dialogue. Her introduction of the gun permit, the constitutional knowledge — it was all setup for this moment. Character-specific and satisfying. The line delivery of 'Get out of my bodega' has to be legendary in context.

jokes.text: Post-mugging: 'Maybe you could've gotten the bag and the money.' / 'Maybe you could've remembered my name.'
jokes.setup: The crisis is over; Abbi thanks the bodega owner
jokes.punchline: The bodega owner uses the moment to extract a final sting — the name thing is still unresolved, and she weaponizes it as a final gotcha
jokes.explanation: This is the best callback in the episode and one of the most structurally satisfying jokes. The bodega owner has just saved Abbi's life. In any other show, this would be a straightforward heroic moment. Instead, she pivots to the unresolved name grievance — 'Maybe you could've gotten the bag AND the money. Maybe you could've remembered my name.' It's perfect because: (1) it's a pure callback with maximum irony — the person who saved her still won't let the name thing go, (2) it humanizes the bodega owner as someone with real dignity who has noticed being anonymized, (3) the structure is impeccable — the 'maybe you could've' parallel construction makes it feel inevitable, (4) 'That's fair' as Abbi's response is the perfect two-word capitulation. This rivals 'Bye, Abbi' as the episode's best structural joke.

jokes.text: 'That's fair.' — Abbi's response to being told she should've remembered her bodega owner's name
jokes.setup: The bodega owner has just saved Abbi's life and used the moment to deliver a pointed observation about name-memory
jokes.punchline: Abbi's 'That's fair' — not defensive, not dismissive, just a flat acknowledgment of legitimate criticism. Immediately after nearly being robbed.
jokes.explanation: Two words doing the full job. 'That's fair' as a post-robbery response is perfect Abbi — self-aware, self-deprecating, constitutionally incapable of arguing with a valid point even in crisis. It closes the name thread with elegant economy.

jokes.text: Ilana calling her mom to come home: 'Ugh, Ilana, no. That's how it spreads. Plus, if I got bed bugs from my sister what would people think?'
jokes.setup: Ilana calls her mom in desperation after being turned away by everyone
jokes.punchline: Her mom refuses — not out of bed bug concern primarily, but because of what people would think about getting bugs from her own daughter — social reputation over family loyalty
jokes.explanation: Good character work establishing Ilana's family dynamic. The 'what would people think' reason is funnier than a straightforward bed bug refusal. Establishes that Ilana's mom shares some of her social anxiety.

jokes.text: 'You are so afraid of incest. You need to talk about that in therapy.'
jokes.setup: Her mom says getting bed bugs from her sister would be embarrassing, seeming squeamish
jokes.punchline: Ilana re-reads the squeamishness as incest-phobia — a complete non-sequitur that reveals Ilana's brain is operating on a completely different plane
jokes.explanation: Only Ilana. The leap from 'I don't want bed bugs from my sister' to 'you're afraid of incest' is so specifically Ilana's mode of thinking — she's constantly sexualizing everything and then weaponizing progressive language ('talk about that in therapy') against anyone who resists. Highly character-specific.

jokes.text: Ilana's mom pivot from refusing to inviting her home: 'We'll go do nails, then bags, then maybe...' immediately followed by Ilana's condition-laden acceptance (all laundry, mostly showering)
jokes.setup: Ilana's mom has just said 'no' and then immediately pivots to 'of course come home'
jokes.punchline: The warm invitation is immediately transactional — Ilana explains she'll need full washer/dryer access and will spend most of the time scrubbing bugs off her body
jokes.explanation: A functional comedic reversal — mom says no, then immediately yes, then Ilana reveals the 'yes' is going to be awful. Middling execution.

jokes.text: Ilana's mom: 'You haven't been home in months and now you get bed bugs and you wanna bring 'em here? No, no, no. Fuck you.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has laid out her bed-bug-recovery demands
jokes.punchline: Her mom processes the full picture and returns to refusal — 'Fuck you' from a parent as the endpoint of the negotiation
jokes.explanation: A parent saying 'fuck you' to their adult child is a reliable comedic punctuation mark. Works here because it follows the warm-then-transactional reversal. The Glazer family dynamic is established as chaotic-loving.

jokes.text: Lincoln refuses Ilana: 'Sorry, Ilana, I can't let you in. I'm a dad now. Sticky's health is my first priority.' / 'You named her?' / 'Yeah, Sticky. Like sticky rice. It's my favorite type of rice.' / 'That's so cute it hurts.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is rejected by Lincoln who cites paternal duty to the dog
jokes.punchline: The dog's name is 'Sticky,' named after Lincoln's favorite type of rice — a completely sincere, dorky, Lincoln-specific explanation that Ilana can't help but find adorable even mid-rejection
jokes.explanation: This is a lovely character joke for Lincoln. 'Sticky rice. It's my favorite type of rice' is so earnestly Lincoln — methodical, food-oriented, uncool. 'That's so cute it hurts' as Ilana's response even while being rejected is very true to their relationship dynamic. High character integration.

jokes.text: Bodega owner talking-head: Second Amendment / Constitution speech — 'I know it like the back of my hand. I'm from Philadelphia. The suburbs of Philadelphia. 40 minutes to the Founding Fathers. Crackin' that bell, right? Liberty! Ding-dong...'
jokes.setup: Massouma is explaining her constitutional knowledge as justification for carrying a gun
jokes.punchline: Her knowledge deteriorates from 'Second Amendment' to 'Founding Fathers' to 'crackin' that bell' to literally making bell sounds — the speech unravels into nonsense the more she elaborates
jokes.explanation: A great slow-unraveling joke. Massouma starts confidently and ends making bell sounds. The escalation — from constitutional citation to suburban geography to ding-dong — is a perfect structure for revealing someone's knowledge is shallower than claimed. Also subverts expectations by applying this to an immigrant character who's claiming constitutional expertise.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I know it like the back of, like... my foot.' / '...because I'm from Philadelphia. The suburbs of Philadelphia.'
jokes.setup: Massouma says she knows the Constitution like the back of her hand; Abbi tries to match the intensity
jokes.punchline: 'Like the back of... my foot' — she was reaching for 'hand' but landed on 'foot,' a small absurdist slip that shows she's performing knowledge she doesn't have
jokes.explanation: Small but sharp. 'The back of my foot' is a perfectly weird malapropism that reveals Abbi is faking constitutional authority. 'Suburbs of Philadelphia' as constitutional credential is also good — the proximity logic is very American.

jokes.text: I know it like the back of, like... my foot. Never forget it. 'Cause I'm from Philadelphia. Well, I'm from the suburbs of Philadelphia. But, like, it's like, 40 minutes tops to the Founding Fathers. Crackin' that bell, right? Liberty! Ding-dong...
jokes.setup: Massouma says she knows the Constitution like the back of her hand; Abbi tries to match this
jokes.punchline: Abbi's constitutional expertise: she's from 'the suburbs of Philadelphia,' '40 minutes tops to the Founding Fathers,' culminating in 'Crackin' that bell, right? Liberty! Ding-dong...' — a complete deterioration of any civic credibility
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating Abbi character comedy. The path from 'back of my foot' (already wrong) to '40 minutes to the Founding Fathers' to 'Liberty! Ding-dong...' is a perfect disintegration. 'Crackin' that bell, right? Ding-dong' applied to the Liberty Bell is both historically ignorant and completely specific to Abbi's suburban Philadelphia experience. The joke rewards the full escalation.

jokes.text: 'What I'm really looking forward to is the 26th Amendment because then I'll actually get to be part of the conversation.' / 'Totally. Conversation Amendment.'
jokes.setup: Massouma is discussing amendments she's looking forward to
jokes.punchline: The 26th Amendment is the voting age amendment (1971 — already ratified decades ago). Massouma seems to think it's forthcoming. Abbi immediately agrees, calling it 'the Conversation Amendment' — neither of them knows what it is
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke that rewards constitutional knowledge. Massouma's anticipation of an already-passed amendment and Abbi's instant agreement with a made-up name for it ('Conversation Amendment') reveals both characters' constitutional ignorance — while Massouma was just presenting herself as an expert. The irony is rich: the woman who referenced the Constitution to justify her gun permit doesn't know it. Well crafted.

jokes.text: Abbi's apology to Massouma: 'I may be a dumb, white bitch, but I'm not that dumb. And really, really, I'm not a bitch.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is apologizing for not knowing Massouma's name
jokes.punchline: She uses 'dumb, white bitch' as a self-descriptor and then immediately tries to walk back each component — 'not that dumb' / 'not a bitch' — leaving 'white' as the only unchallenged descriptor
jokes.explanation: Smart, self-aware comedy. The punchline is implicit — she rebuts 'dumb' and 'bitch' but leaves 'white' standing, which is the thing she can't argue. The structure of rebutting two-thirds of your own self-insult is clever and the line reads as genuine Abbi — earnest, self-flagellating, not fully in control of what she's saying.

jokes.text: 'I mean, he had a gun. And then he's ragging on me about my balance? I'm like, you're mugging me, bitch. What a bitch.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is recounting the mugging to Ilana
jokes.punchline: She's retroactively self-righteous about the mugging — calling the mugger 'a bitch' and centering the grievance not on being robbed but on having her finances criticized
jokes.explanation: A good callback that lets Abbi process the mugging in retrospect. The priority inversion — being angrier about the financial critique than the gun — is a nice character beat.

jokes.text: Abbi arrives at Anthropologie in the security vest with a walkie-talkie: 'All's clear at the west door.' / 'Do the thing.' / 'Abbi out.'
jokes.setup: Abbi did a great interview and believed she was getting a creative role at Anthropologie — designing window displays, pulling fabrics
jokes.punchline: She's been hired as a security guard. The window-display sketches are pointedly ignored. She's now doing walkie-talkie check-ins.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying structural payoff. The episode has spent considerable time building Abbi's confidence in the interview — her speech about climbing the corporate ladder, her window-display sketches — all to end here: 'All's clear at the west door.' The vest is the visual punchline to the whole sequence. 'Do the thing' / 'Abbi out' as the endpoint of her aspirational interview arc is perfectly deflating without being cruel. Strong callback structure.

jokes.text: Abbi, you okay? Yeah, uh, all's clear at the west door.
jokes.setup: Abbi is now visibly a security guard at Anthropologie
jokes.punchline: Ilana asks if she's okay; Abbi responds in security guard protocol — 'all's clear at the west door' — she's already been absorbed into the job identity
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — Abbi's immediate adoption of security guard lingo reveals both her coping mechanism (commitment to the bit) and her defeat. 'All's clear at the west door' as a response to 'you okay?' is a great character moment.

jokes.text: Post-credits: 'You ever consider sex work? Like maybe small stuff. Hand jobs.' / 'Would they have to see anything other than my hand?' / 'No.' / 'I would do that if it was just my hand. Let me get back to you.'
jokes.setup: Post-credits scene; Ilana and Abbi are broke and in crisis
jokes.punchline: Abbi's genuine consideration of the hand-job-only clause — and her actual willingness to follow up on it — is both the joke and a perfect character beat
jokes.explanation: Strong post-credits joke. Abbi's negotiation of the minimum viable sex work — 'just the hand, no visibility' — and her earnest 'let me get back to you' is quintessential Broad City: raunchy, pragmatic, and surprisingly sweet in its practicality. The 'let me get back to you' implies she's actually going to think about this. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: You ever consider sex work? Like, maybe small stuff. Hand jobs. / Would they have to see anything other than my hand? / No. / I would do that if it was just my hand.
jokes.setup: Post-episode tag: they're discussing financial solutions to their poverty
jokes.punchline: Abbi's extremely specific condition for sex work — only if it's just her hand — and Ilana's deadpan confirmation that yes, hands-only is a thing, followed by Abbi genuinely considering it
jokes.explanation: Excellent post-credits tag. The joke is Abbi's precise parameters for acceptable sex work — the hand boundary — and her genuine openness to it once confirmed. 'I would do that if it was just my hand' is perfectly in character: Abbi's pragmatic problem-solving applied to a very Broad City scenario. 'Let me get back to you' from Ilana is the right non-conclusion.

jokes.text: BTS segment: Arturo Castro (Jaime) standing fully clothed in the shower: 'I've just been in the shower with all of my clothes on. Let's start the water and let's roll sound. Hoo! Okay baby! We have bedbugs!'
jokes.setup: Behind-the-scenes footage; Arturo introduces the episode's bed bug shower scene
jokes.punchline: He is literally standing in the shower fully clothed, having apparently been there for a while, and does a method-actor-style commitment bit before the scene rolls
jokes.explanation: A charming BTS gag. The commitment to being in the shower while doing a talking-head interview is a good visual joke. Not scripted comedy but works as making-of humor.
